name: Early-universe initial conditions (horizon, flatness, structure)
summary: The observed universe is homogeneous on scales that were never in causal contact under standard FRW expansion (horizon problem); spatially flat to high precision despite curvature being radiatively unstable in expansion (flatness problem); and seeded with nearly scale-invariant adiabatic perturbations of unknown origin (structure problem). Without an extra mechanism, all three coincidences require finely-tuned initial conditions at the Planck scale.

characteristic_numbers.note: Spatial curvature is observed flat to <0.5%; under FRW evolution alone, deviations grow with time, so the early universe must have been flat to ~10^60 at the Planck epoch.

characteristic_numbers.expected: uncorrelated across patches outside causal horizon
characteristic_numbers.note: Patches separated by more than the recombination-era horizon are observed to have correlated temperature; they had no causal contact in standard FRW.
Near-scale-invariance with slight red tilt, consistent with single-field slow-roll inflation.

principles_stressed.note: The three coincidences are not separately statistically improbable; they all reflect that FRW evolution does not naturally produce the observed initial state. Inflation closes them all simultaneously — a paradigm dot-alignment example.

history: Horizon and flatness problems articulated by Dicke and Peebles (1979) at the Einstein Centenary symposium. Inflationary cosmology proposed by Guth (1981) to address them; quickly extended to slow-roll models by Linde (1982) and Albrecht-Steinhardt (1982). Subsequent decades produced thousands of inflationary models; observational constraints (Planck, BICEP/Keck) have closed many but the framework itself remains the dominant paradigm.

references.citation: Guth, 'The Inflationary Universe: A Possible Solution to the Horizon and Flatness Problems', Phys. Rev. D 23, 347 (1981)
Planck Collaboration, 'Planck 2018 results. X. Constraints on inflation', A&A 641, A10 (2020)
